critic_raw:
Issue 1: Endpoint drift — LSTM on 24h severe-sepsis deterioration, transformer on 12h vasopressor onset. Non-comparable prediction targets.

Issue 2: Patient-level leakage — encounter-level random splitting in a 5-year dataset with recurring ICU patients places the same patients in both training and holdout partitions.

Issue 3: Timestamp mismatch — LSTM validated with EHR-charted lab timestamps; transformer trained with specimen collection time when available. This shifts feature availability earlier for the transformer.

Issue 4: Cohort unit mismatch — LSTM uses individual ADT admissions; transformer uses merged cross-transfer episodes. Different units of analysis produce non-comparable metrics.
defender_raw:
Issues 1, 2, 3, and 4 are all conceded at a structural level. The comparison changes the endpoint, the timestamp convention, the cohort construction rule, and the split grain simultaneously. These are legitimate benchmarking concerns.

The defense position is: (1) the transformer is still worth testing, as shown by the prespecified Hospital B external validation; (2) the lactate and vasopressor features are pre-prediction-time and are not target leakage; (3) the external validation at Hospital B provides independent evidence that the transformer is capturing real signal.

A harmonized rerun on a shared cohort with one endpoint, one timestamp policy, one cohort unit definition, and patient-level chronological splitting is the appropriate next step. The current benchmark does not support a clean promotion claim but does support a structured rerun.
adjudication_raw:
Round 1:
Critic raises 4 issues. Defender concedes all four at a structural level, defends the pre-prediction-time feature handling and Hospital B external validation.

All four points are effectively resolved in Round 1 as empirical_test_required — the defender agrees a harmonized rerun is needed on all four dimensions.

Round 2 (forced):
No new arguments — the defender reiterates that a structured rerun is the appropriate path and that the Hospital B external results support giving the transformer a fair test. Critic does not introduce new issues.

All 4 points resolved. Forced round is hollow (no new resolutions — same verdict as Round 1).

Final verdict: empirical_test_agreed.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: endpoint changed from 24h severe-sepsis to 12h vasopressor onset
Issue 2: encounter-level random split leaks recurring ICU patients across partitions
Issue 3: lab timestamp changed from EHR-charted to specimen collection time
Issue 4: cohort unit changed from individual admissions to merged cross-transfer episodes

empirical_test.condition: Rebuild shared cohort with one endpoint, one timestamp policy, one cohort construction rule, patient-level chronological split; rerun both models
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: AUROC edge falls below 0.01 and recall advantage disappears on harmonized cohorts
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer leads by at least 0.02 AUROC and 5+ recall points at same alert rate on both hospitals
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Modest AUROC gain persists but recall advantage disappears, or gains inconsistent across hospitals
